ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for stir-fried broccoli with prawn or scallop; closest defensible match is the generic stir-fried vegetable dish class FOODON_03315702.
ontology.foodex2_description: Broccoli, cooked — used as the primary vegetable component anchor; seafood component (prawn/scallop) is an additional ingredient not separately coded in this composite dish entry.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for stir-fried broccoli with prawn or scallop. A0CKP (cooked broccoli) is used as the primary vegetable anchor. Seafood component acknowledged as additional ingredient.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite stir-fried dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for prepared composite vegetable-seafood dishes of this type.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of stir-fried broccoli with prawn or scallop is approximately 400 g, typical of a shared side dish or individual main at a Singaporean Chinese restaurant or hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Broccoli with Prawn — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Foodo record F14080040 at confidence 0.95. Secondary micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were present in the input record attributed to a non-approved delivery platform source; these have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central composite estimates for stir-fried broccoli with seafood (USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked broccoli and cooked shrimp/scallop) and retained where plausible. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients set to -1 where no approved-source data is available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for non-starchy vegetables with seafood
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried broccoli with prawn or scallop. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for broccoli (GI ~15, low-GI non-starchy vegetable) as the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient. Prawn and scallop contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially affect GI. Oyster sauce and soy sauce contribute minor carbohydrate but insufficient to elevate GI category above low. GL per serving calculated as: GI (15) × carbohydrate per serving (10.6 g from 400 g × 2.65 g/100g) / 100 = 1.6. Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283 used as reference framework.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 226 mg per 100 g; a 400 g plate delivers approximately 904 mg sodium, representing ~39% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced oyster sauce and soy sauce to lower sodium content below 150 mg per 100 g, and consider low-sodium soy sauce alternatives to keep a full plate serving under 600 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.65 g per 100 g), zero sugar, 1.5 g dietary fibre, and a GI of approximately 15 produce a glycemic load of only 1.6 per 400 g plate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is well-suited for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; the low glycemic load and high protein content from prawn or scallop support satiety and glycaemic stability, though cornstarch-thickened sauces should be kept minimal to preserve the low-carbohydrate profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 0.75 g per 100 g and trans fat is zero, but sodium at 226 mg per 100 g is a concern for cardiovascular risk at full plate portions.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat, zero trans fat, and lean seafood protein make this dish broadly heart-healthy, but individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size or request sauce reduction to keep sodium intake within cardiovascular-safe limits of under 1500 mg per day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Broccoli provides sulforaphane and vitamin C (17.5 mg per 100 g); ginger contributes gingerols with anti-inflammatory properties; scallop or prawn provides lean protein with minimal saturated fat.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish supports anti-inflammatory dietary patterns relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management; reducing oyster sauce and soy sauce will further lower sodium and reduce risk of RA-related fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Broccoli with Prawn or Scallop is a popular Singaporean Chinese vegetable-seafood dish consisting of fresh broccoli florets wok-fried at high heat with either prawns or scallops, aromatics such as garlic and ginger, and a savoury sauce typically made from oyster sauce, light soy sauce, and a small amount of sesame oil. The dish is finished with a light cornstarch slurry to create a glossy coating. The texture is crisp-tender broccoli with succulent, lightly caramelised seafood. The flavour profile is umami-forward, mildly savoury, and fragrant from the wok hei (breath of the wok). Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 56 kcal, 4 g protein, 3.25 g fat (0.75 g saturated), 2.65 g carbohydrate, zero sugar, 1.5 g dietary fibre, and 226 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is a good source of vitamin C (17.5 mg per 100 g) from broccoli and provides lean seafood protein. Clinically, it is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular health due to its low carbohydrate, low saturated fat, and zero trans fat profile, though the sodium content warrants attention for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients at full plate portions. It is dairy-free and halal-compatible when prepared with halal-certified sauces.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo canonical record F14080040 at 0.95 confidence. Secondary micronutrients cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked broccoli and cooked shrimp/scallop and found plausible. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for broccoli as the dominant carbohydrate source. Multilingual names verified for Singaporean Chinese cuisine category.
notes: 1. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source for this preparation; no hydrogenated fats used in standard stir-fry preparation. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source; oyster sauce contributes negligible sugar at typical usage levels within this composite dish. All core macros (protein_g 4.0, fat_g 3.25, carbohydrate_g 2.65) are non-zero and sourced from HPB. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, mineral panel, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite stir-fried dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database record F14080040 or in USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026. 3. Input record dietary_tags included 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' which are incorrect given the presence of prawn and scallop; these tags have been removed. 4. Calcium (20 mg), iron (0.5 mg), cholesterol (25 mg), saturated fat (0.75 g), trans fat (0.0 g), and vitamin C (17.5 mg) were attributed to a non-approved delivery platform source in the input; these values have been retained after cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked broccoli (USDA FDC ID 170379) and cooked shrimp (USDA FDC ID 175180) confirming plausibility. 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on broccoli GI from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources.
